content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
133

10	/-!
11	# The identity component of the Picard scheme (A.3)
12	
13	This file is the **A.3** sub-build chapter for the project's positive-genus arm
14	of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It scaffolds the abstract identity-component
15	substrate for a `k`-group scheme locally of finite type and specialises it to
16	`G = Pic_{C/k}`, packaging:
17	
18	1. the open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure of the identity component,
19	2. the degree map `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`,
20	3. the abelian-variety identification of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` (the Jacobian variety
21	   of `C` when `C/k` is a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
22	   positive genus).
23	
24	## Status (iter-185 NEW file-skeleton)
25	
26	This file is the **iter-185 IdentityComponent** file-skeleton (NEW lane,
27	blueprint-reviewer HARD GATE cleared for the iter-184 plan-phase chapter
28	`Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`). Each of the five blueprint-pinned
29	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
30	`\lean{...}` pin in `Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`) with a `sorry` body. The
31	bodies are iter-186+ work; iter-185's mandate is the mechanical scaffold only.
32	
33	The 5 pinned declarations are:
34	
35	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent` (def, ~5 LOC) — the
36	   **identity component** `G^0` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite
37	   type, as a `k`-scheme (an `Over (Spec k)`-object). Abstract substrate
38	   reusable outside the Picard context; not yet in Mathlib.
39	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`
40	   (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the bundled statement that `G^0` is an open and
41	   closed subscheme of `G` via an open immersion `G^0 ↪ G`.
42	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme` (def, ~5 LOC) — the **identity
43	   component of the Picard scheme** `Pic⁰_{C/k}`, obtained by applying
44	   `IdentityComponent` to `G = PicScheme C`.
45	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree` (def, ~5 LOC) — the **degree
46	   map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`, extracting the leading coefficient of the
47	   Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed
48	   degree-one polarisation `O_C(1)`.
49	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
50	   — the **abelian-variety identification** of `Pic⁰_{C/k}`: smooth, proper,
51	   geometrically irreducible `k`-group scheme of dimension `g(C)` --- the
52	   Jacobian variety of `C`.
53	
54	## Note on type expressivity
55	
56	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
57	pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
58	
59	- `IdentityComponent G : Over (Spec (.of k))` — a `k`-scheme, not the
60	  tautological `G` itself.
61	- `IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme G` — asserts the *existence* of an
62	  open immersion morphism `IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` whose underlying map of
63	  schemes is both an open and a closed immersion (clopen subscheme).
64	- `Pic0Scheme C : Over (Spec (.of k))` — a `k`-scheme.
65	- `PicScheme.degree C : (Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left) → ℤ` —
66	  a genuine function from `k`-points to `ℤ`, not a constant.
67	- `Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety C` — asserts the conjunction of the four
68	  abelian-variety properties (proper, smooth, geometrically irreducible,
69	  group-object structure); not vacuous because each conjunct is a genuine
70	  property/structure on the (typed-sorry) `Pic0Scheme C`.
71	
72	## References
73

75	5 pins). Sources:
76	- Kleiman, "The Picard scheme", §5, Lem.~`lem:agps` (identity component
77	  substrate) + Prp.~`prp:pic0` (specialisation to `Pic_{C/k}`) +

79	  (smoothness/dimension) + Ex.~`ex:jac` + Rmk.~`rmk:Jac`
80	  (arXiv:math/0504020 pp. 36, 38, 47, 50–51);
81	- Milne, "Abelian Varieties" (course notes, 2008), §III.1
82	  (def. of abelian variety, p. 8; dimension equals genus, Rmk. III.1.4(e),
83	  p. 86).

88	universe u

90	open CategoryTheory Limits
91	
92	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
93	
94	/-! ## §1. The identity component of a group scheme — abstract substrate
95	
96	The identity component is an abstract feature of `k`-group schemes locally of
97	finite type. We package the definition + open-and-closed-subgroup-scheme
98	structure in the `GroupScheme` namespace so they are reusable outside the
99	Picard context.
100	
101	In this project, a "`k`-group scheme" is encoded as an
102	`Over (Spec (.of k))`-object `G` carrying a `[GrpObj G]` instance (the
103	group-object structure in the over-category). The locally-of-finite-type
104	hypothesis is the `[LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom]` instance on the structural
105	morphism to the base.
106	
107	Blueprint references: `def:identity_component_group_scheme` +
108	`thm:identity_component_open_subgroup` (Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`). -/
109	
110	namespace GroupScheme
111	
112	/-- Helper (iter-186): the **clopen carrier `Set`** of the identity
113	component of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite type, packaged as a
114	`G.left.Opens`. The intended substantive identity is: this is the (Zariski-)
115	open subset of `|G|` whose underlying set is the connected component of `|G|`
116	through the image of the identity section `e : Spec k → G`.
117	
118	The body is a *typed* `sorry` carrying Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3)
119	substantive content:
120	
121	* the carrier set is `connectedComponent x` for `x = e(*)` the image of
122	  the identity section (well-defined because `Spec k` is a singleton);
123	* openness is EGA I 6.1.9 — connected components of a locally Noetherian
124	  topological space are open. The relevant Mathlib hook is
125	  `isOpen_connectedComponent` (which needs a `LocallyConnectedSpace`
126	  instance on `G.left.toTopCat`; the implication "locally Noetherian
127	  topological space ⟹ locally connected" is a Mathlib gap at the current
128	  pinned commit and is the substantive content of this helper).
129	
130	Downstream consumers (`IdentityComponent`, `IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`)
131	plug this typed sorry into their bodies and propagate the typed-sorry
132	discharge cleanly. -/
133	private noncomputable def identityComponentCarrier {k : Type u} [Field k]
134	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
135	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
136	    G.left.Opens :=
137	  sorry
138	
139	/-- The **identity component** `G^0` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of
140	finite type.
141	
142	Encoded as a `k`-scheme: an object of `Over (Spec (.of k))`, carrying the
143	intended substantive identity "this is the connected component of `|G|`
144	containing the identity section `e`, equipped with the open-subscheme
145	structure inherited from `G`". The associated open immersion
146	`IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` is the content of
147	`IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`.
148	
149	iter-186 body: built from the `identityComponentCarrier G` helper — the
150	open subscheme of `G` whose underlying topological space is the connected
151	component of `|G|` through the image of the identity section. The
152	structure morphism is the inherited `(identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom`.
153	The substantive content (the actual carrier `Set`, plus its openness from
154	EGA I 6.1.9: locally Noetherian spaces have open connected components)
155	lives in the typed-sorry body of `identityComponentCarrier`. -/
156	noncomputable def IdentityComponent {k : Type u} [Field k]
157	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
158	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
159	    Over (Spec (.of k)) :=
160	  Over.mk ((identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom)
161	
162	/-- **The identity component is an open and closed subgroup scheme.**
163	
164	The bundled statement of Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3): the identity
165	component `G^0 = IdentityComponent G` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of
166	finite type comes with a morphism `IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` (in
167	`Over (Spec (.of k))`) whose underlying scheme morphism is both an open
168	immersion and a closed immersion (i.e. the inclusion of a clopen
169	subscheme).
170	
171	The full Kleiman conclusion also packages the group-subscheme property (the
172	inclusion is a homomorphism of `k`-group schemes), finite-type-ness over `k`
173	(`LocallyOfFiniteType` + quasi-compactness), geometric irreducibility, and
174	base-change-commutation. Those refinements live as separate instances /
175	follow-up lemmas in iter-186+; the file-skeleton pins only the clopen
176	open-immersion conclusion as a Nonempty-witness.
177	
178	iter-186 body: the inclusion morphism is `Over.homMk (identityComponentCarrier G).ι`,
179	with the over-category compatibility holding by definition of
180	`IdentityComponent G`. The `.left` of this morphism is
181	`(identityComponentCarrier G).ι`, an open immersion by the global
182	`Scheme.Opens.instIsOpenImmersionι` instance. For the closed-immersion
183	half we apply `IsClosedImmersion.of_isPreimmersion` to the open immersion
184	and reduce to `IsClosed (↑(identityComponentCarrier G) : Set _)`. The
185	remaining inline `sorry` is the substantive content: the carrier of
186	`identityComponentCarrier G` is closed, by "closure of a connected subspace
187	is connected" applied to the connected component through the image of the
188	identity section. -/
189	theorem IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme {k : Type u} [Field k]
190	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
191	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
192	    Nonempty {f : IdentityComponent G ⟶ G //
193	        IsOpenImmersion f.left ∧ IsClosedImmersion f.left} := by
194	  -- Over-category compatibility for the underlying open immersion.
195	  have hcomp : (identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom = (IdentityComponent G).hom := by
196	    simp [IdentityComponent]
197	  -- The over-morphism witness.
198	  let f : IdentityComponent G ⟶ G :=
199	    Over.homMk (U := IdentityComponent G) (V := G) (identityComponentCarrier G).ι hcomp
200	  refine ⟨⟨f, ?_, ?_⟩⟩
201	  · -- `f.left = (identityComponentCarrier G).ι` (definitionally; `Over.homMk_left` simp).
202	    -- An open immersion by `Scheme.Opens.instIsOpenImmersionι`.
203	    change IsOpenImmersion (identityComponentCarrier G).ι
204	    infer_instance
205	  · -- `f.left = (identityComponentCarrier G).ι` is a preimmersion (open immersion ⟹
206	    -- immersion ⟹ preimmersion). Its range is the carrier set (`Scheme.Opens.range_ι`),
207	    -- and closure of that connected subset is itself (Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3):
208	    -- the connected component through the identity is closed in `|G|`).
209	    change IsClosedImmersion (identityComponentCarrier G).ι
210	    apply IsClosedImmersion.of_isPreimmersion
211	    rw [Scheme.Opens.range_ι]
212	    -- Substantive sorry: closedness of the (open) connected component of `|G|` through
213	    -- the identity section — the "closure of a connected subspace is connected"
214	    -- argument in Kleiman's proof of lem:agps~(3) paragraph 1.
215	    sorry
216	
217	end GroupScheme
218	
219	/-! ## §2. The identity component of the Picard scheme
220	
221	We specialise the abstract identity-component substrate to
222	`G = PicScheme C`, the Picard scheme of a smooth proper geometrically
223	integral curve `C/k` (from sibling `Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean`).
224	
225	Blueprint reference: `def:pic_zero_subscheme` (Kleiman §5 opening + Prp.
226	`prp:pic0`). -/
227	
228	namespace Scheme
229	
230	/-- The **identity component of the Picard scheme** `Pic⁰_{C/k}`.
231	
232	Defined as the identity component
233	`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent (PicScheme C)` of the Picard scheme
234	`Pic_{C/k}` (from sibling `Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean`,
235	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme`). By
236	`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`, `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an
237	open and closed subgroup scheme of `Pic_{C/k}` of finite type over `k`,
238	geometrically irreducible, and its formation commutes with extension of the
239	base field.
240	
241	iter-186+: the body unwinds to `GroupScheme.IdentityComponent (PicScheme C)`
242	once the `[LocallyOfFiniteType (PicScheme C).hom]` instance lands on
243	`PicScheme C` (it follows from the Kleiman §4 structure: `Pic_{C/k}` is
244	locally of finite type as the disjoint union of open quasi-projective
245	`k`-subschemes). For the iter-185 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
246	noncomputable def Pic0Scheme {k : Type u} [Field k]
247	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
248	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
249	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
250	    Over (Spec (.of k)) :=
251	  sorry
252	
253	/-! ## §3. The degree map
254	
255	The disjoint-union structure of `Pic_{C/k}` (a disjoint union of open
256	quasi-projective `k`-subschemes, indexed by Hilbert polynomial via
257	`PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient`) stratifies its `T`-points by the leading
258	coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
259	relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation. On `k`-points this gives the
260	**degree map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`.
261	
262	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree_pic` (Milne III.1, p.~88). -/
263	
264	namespace PicScheme
265	
266	/-- The **degree map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`.
267	
268	Sends a `k`-point `λ ∈ Pic_{C/k}(k)` --- a morphism
269	`Spec k ⟶ (PicScheme C).left` --- to the leading coefficient of the
270	Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf `L` on `C` (relative
271	to a fixed degree-one polarisation `O_C(1)`). By Riemann--Roch,
272	`χ(C, L ⊗ O_C(n)) = n · deg L + 1 - g`, so the degree is the leading
273	coefficient of `Φ_L(n)`, well-defined on the isomorphism class `[L]` and on
274	the `k`-point `λ` (because `PicScheme C` represents the étale-sheafified
275	relative Picard functor).
276	
277	The degree map is a group homomorphism for the additive structure on
278	`Pic_{C/k}(k)` (tensor product on `L`) and the standard `(ℤ, +)`. The full
279	group-homomorphism refinement / functoriality in `k` lives as a follow-up
280	lemma in iter-186+; the file-skeleton pins only the underlying function.
281	
282	iter-186+: the body extracts the representing invertible sheaf from
283	`(PicScheme.representable C)`, forms its Hilbert polynomial via the project's
284	Hilbert-polynomial machinery (sibling `Picard/QuotScheme.lean`), and returns
285	the leading coefficient. For the iter-185 file-skeleton the body is a typed
286	`sorry`. -/
287	noncomputable def degree {k : Type u} [Field k]
288	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
289	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
290	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
291	    (Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left) → ℤ :=
292	  sorry
293	
294	end PicScheme
295	
296	/-! ## §4. `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an abelian variety
297	
298	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies `Pic⁰_{C/k}` with an
299	abelian variety of dimension `g(C)` --- the Jacobian variety of `C`. In
300	this project, "abelian variety" is the conjunction of the four properties
301	`[GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]`
302	threaded through `AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity` and consumed by
303	`AlgebraicJacobian.Albanese.AlbaneseUP`.
304	
305	Blueprint reference: `thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety` (Kleiman §5
306	Ex.~`ex:jac` + Rmk.~`rmk:Jac`; cf. Milne §I.1, Rmk. III.1.4(e)). -/
307	
308	namespace Pic0Scheme
309	
310	/-- **`Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an abelian variety.**
311	
312	For `C/k` a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus `g = g(C)`
313	over a field `k`, the identity component `Pic⁰_{C/k}` of the Picard scheme
314	is an *abelian variety over* `k`: a smooth, proper, geometrically
315	irreducible `k`-group scheme. (Commutativity follows automatically from
316	Milne §I.1, Cor. 1.4, and is not separately stated here.)
317	
318	The bundled statement: the four pieces `[IsProper] ∧ [Smooth] ∧
319	[GeometricallyIrreducible] ∧ Nonempty (GrpObj _)` hold on
320	`(Pic0Scheme C).hom` and `(Pic0Scheme C)` respectively.
321	
322	Dimension `g = g(C)`: not stated separately at the file-skeleton level
323	(it requires `dim_k (Pic0Scheme C).left = AlgebraicGeometry.genus C`, which
324	involves the Krull-dimension API on the underlying scheme); iter-186+
325	follow-up.
326	
327	iter-186+: the body assembles the four conjuncts from
328	- `GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme` (clopen subscheme of
329	  `PicScheme C`),
330	- Kleiman §5 Thm.~`th:qpp&p` (`X/k` projective + geom. integral ⟹
331	  `Pic⁰_{X/k}` quasi-projective; upgrade to projective when geom. normal,
332	  which holds for smooth proper curves of positive genus),
333	- Kleiman §5 Cor.~`cor:sm` + Ex.~`ex:jac` (smoothness of `Pic_{X/k}` at the
334	  identity for smooth proper curves; hence smooth of dimension
335	  `dim_k H¹(C, O_C) = g(C)` everywhere by uniform smoothness on the open
336	  identity component).
337	For the iter-185 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
338	theorem isAbelianVariety {k : Type u} [Field k]
339	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
340	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
341	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
342	    IsProper (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧ Smooth (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧
343	      GeometricallyIrreducible (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧
344	      Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C)) :=
345	  sorry
346	
347	end Pic0Scheme
348	
349	end Scheme

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
66	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
67	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
68	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
70	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
71	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
73	  %
74	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
77	  % reduced open subscheme.
78	  %
79	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
80	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
81	  % extending \(k\).
82	  %  \end{lem}"
83	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
88	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}
94	
95	% The four conclusions of Kleiman \S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3) are split below
96	% into per-conclusion theorem blocks, each with its own \lean{...} pin to
97	% a Lean declaration. The verbatim source quote of lem:agps~(3) is
98	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} below
99	% (covering all four conclusions); the three subsequent blocks reference
100	% that quote rather than duplicating it. The combined proof, sharing the
101	% \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment from Kleiman's proof, follows
102	% the fourth block.
103	
104	\begin{theorem}\leanok
105	[Identity component is a clopen subscheme]
106	  \label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
108	
109	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
110	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
111	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % " \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
114	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
115	  % extending \(k\)."
116	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
117	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
118	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)
119	  (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is an open and closed
120	  subscheme of \(G\): the inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is both an
121	  open immersion and a closed immersion of schemes.
122	\end{theorem}
123	
124	\begin{theorem}
125	[Identity component inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism]
126	  \label{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
127	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
128	
129	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
130	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
131	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
132	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
133	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
134	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
135	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
136	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
137	  type. The clopen inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\)
138	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is a homomorphism of
139	  \(k\)-group schemes: \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure
140	  from \(G\), and the inclusion morphism in the over-category over
141	  \(\Spec k\) is compatible with the group laws on source and target.
142	\end{theorem}
143	
144	\begin{theorem}
145	[Identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible]
146	  \label{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
148	
149	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
150	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
151	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
152	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
153	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
154	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
155	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
156	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
157	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\) is of finite type over
158	  \(k\) --- equivalently, locally of finite type \emph{and}
159	  quasi-compact --- and geometrically irreducible: after base change
160	  to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the underlying scheme
161	  \(G^0_{\bar k}\) is an irreducible \(\bar k\)-scheme.
162	\end{theorem}
163	
164	\begin{theorem}
165	[Formation of the identity component commutes with base change]
166	  \label{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
167	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
168	
169	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
170	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
171	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
172	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
173	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
174	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
175	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
176	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
177	  type, and \(K/k\) a field extension. The natural map
178	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\), induced by base-changing the inclusion of
179	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} along \(\Spec K \to \Spec k\)
180	  and using the universal property of the identity component on
181	  \(G_K\), is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
182	\end{theorem}
183	
184	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
185	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
186	% (3).)
187	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
188	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
189	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
190	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
191	% \(G^0\) is open too.
192	%
193	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
194	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
195	% extending \(k\).
196	%
197	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
198	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
199	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
200	%
201	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
202	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
203	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
204	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
205	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
206	% [\dots]
207	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
208	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
209	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
210	% [\dots]
211	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
212	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
213	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
214	\begin{proof}
215	  We establish
216	  \crefrange{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
217	  together, following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3). The four
218	  paragraphs below treat, in order: the clopen-subscheme structure
219	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}); base-change commutation
220	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}); the subgroup
221	  homomorphism (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism});
222	  and finite type plus geometric irreducibility
223	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).
224	
225	  \emph{Clopen subscheme (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}).}
226	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
227	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
228	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
229	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
230	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
231	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
232	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
233	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.
234	
235	  \emph{Base-change commutation
236	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}).}
237	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
238	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
239	  particular for any field extension \(K/k\) the base change
240	  \((G^0)_K\) is connected, contains the image of the identity section
241	  of \(G_K\), and is open and closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and
242	  closed-immersion both descend under base change). Hence
243	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
244	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
245	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
246	
247	  \emph{Subgroup homomorphism
248	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}).}
249	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
250	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
251	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
252	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
253	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group
254	  operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
255	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes,
256	  and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\).
257	
258	  \emph{Finite type and geometric irreducibility
259	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).}
260	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
261	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
262	  commutes with field extension by the previous paragraph). Over
263	  \(\overline k\), the product
264	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
265	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
266	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
267	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
268	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
269	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
270	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
271	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
272	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
273	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
274	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
275	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
276	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
277	\end{proof}
278	
279	\section{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
280	\label{sec:pic0_definition}
281	
282	We now specialise the abstract identity-component construction to
283	\(G = \Pic_{C/k}\).
284	
285	\begin{definition}\leanok
286	[The identity component of the Picard scheme]
287	  \label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}
288	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
289	  
290	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening + Prp.~prp:pic0;
291	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
292	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2836-L2841 +
293	  % L2921-L2929).
294	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
295	  % "Having treated the existence of the Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\), we now
296	  % turn to its structure.  In this section, we study the union
297	  % \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of the connected components of the identity element,
298	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\), for \(s\in S\)."
299	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
300	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
301	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
302	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
303	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
304	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
305	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
306	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
307	  % \(k\).
308	  %  \end{prp}"
309	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening +
310	  Prp.~prp:pic0.}
311	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
312	  \(k\). The \emph{identity component of the Picard scheme}
313	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the identity component
314	  \((\Pic_{C/k})^0\) of the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of
315	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}, in the sense of
316	  \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}. By
317	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open
318	  and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of finite type over \(k\),
319	  geometrically irreducible, and its formation commutes with
320	  extension of the base field.
321	\end{definition}
322	
323	\section{The degree map}
324	\label{sec:pic_degree}
325	
326	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
327	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
328	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
329	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
330	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
331	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
332	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
333	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
334	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
335	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
336	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
337	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
338	degree.
339	
340	\begin{definition}\leanok
341	[Degree of a Picard class]
342	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
343	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
344	  
345	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
346	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
347	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
348	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
349	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
350	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
351	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
352	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
353	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
354	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
355	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
356	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
357	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
358	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
359	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
360	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
361	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
362	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
363	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
364	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
365	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
366	  \[
367	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
368	  \]
369	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
370	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
371	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
372	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
373	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
374	  \[
375	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
376	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
377	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
378	  \]
379	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
380	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
381	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
382	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
383	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
384	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
385	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
386	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
387	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
388	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
389	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
390	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
391	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
392	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
393	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
394	  of degree \(d\).
395	\end{definition}
396	
397	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
398	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
399	
400	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
401	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
402	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
403	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
404	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
405	automatically.
406	
407	% The three conclusions of the original ``\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian
408	% variety of dimension \(g\)'' theorem are split below into per-conclusion
409	% blocks, each with its own \lean{...} pin. The Kleiman ex:jac + rmk:Jac
410	% verbatim source quote (covering the abelian-variety structure) is
411	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}; the Milne
412	% III.1.4(e) verbatim quote (covering the dimension formula) is at
413	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}; the Milne III.1 quote
414	% characterising \(\Pic^0(C)\) as degree-zero classes is already reproduced
415	% in \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} and referenced from
416	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}. The combined proof,
417	% sharing the \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment, follows the third
418	% block.
419	
420	\begin{theorem}\leanok
421	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
422	  \label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}
423	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
424	
425	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
426	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~50--51 (read from
427	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3911-L3917 +
428	  % L3990-L3996).
429	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
430	  % "\begin{ex}\label{ex:jac}
431	  % Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat, its geometric fibers are integral
432	  % curves of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Show the
433	  % ``generalized Jacobians'' \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
434	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\).  And show this
435	  % family is projective if and only if \(X/S\) is smooth.
436	  % \end{ex}"
437	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
438	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
439	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
440	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
441	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
442	  % Exercise~REF."
443	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
444	  Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'',
445	  \S I.1 (def.\ of abelian variety, p.~8).}
446	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
447	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) of
448	  \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} is an \emph{abelian variety over
449	  \(k\)}: it is proper, smooth, and geometrically irreducible as a
450	  \(k\)-scheme, and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme structure. (In the
451	  Lean encoding this is the conjunction of
452	  \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]}, and
453	  \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
454	  \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with the existence of a
455	  \texttt{[GrpObj]} structure on \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). Commutativity
456	  follows automatically from Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4 and is not
457	  separately stated.)
458	\end{theorem}
459	
460	\begin{theorem}
461	[Dimension of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals the genus of \(C\)]
462	  \label{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}
463	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
464	
465	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
466	  % \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
467	  % PDF p.~92).
468	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
469	  % "(e) The dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\). If \(C\) has genus zero,
470	  % then \(\Jac(C) = 0\) (this is obvious, because \(\Pic^0(C) = 0\), even
471	  % when one goes to the algebraic closure). If \(C\) has genus 1, then
472	  % \(\Jac(C) = C\) (provided \(C\) has a rational point; otherwise it differs
473	  % from \(C\) --- because \(\Jac(C)\) always has a point)."
474	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
475	  \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86; cf.\ [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'',
476	  \S 5, Cor.~cor:sm + Ex.~ex:jac.}
477	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
478	  \(g = g(C)\) (\cref{def:genus}) over a field \(k\). The dimension of
479	  the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
480	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) equals the genus:
481	  \[
482	    \dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} \;=\; g(C).
483	  \]
484	\end{theorem}
485	
486	\begin{theorem}
487	[\(k\)-points of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) are the kernel of the degree map]
488	  \label{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}
489	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
490	
491	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
492	  % \S III.1, p.~88; verbatim quote reproduced at
493	  % \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} (read from
494	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~94).
495	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
496	  \S III.1, p.~88 (``\(\Pic^0(C)\)\dots\ degree zero''); the verbatim
497	  source quote is at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.}
498	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
499	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point
500	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of
501	  the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
502	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
503	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), specialised in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme})
504	  if and only if the degree
505	  \(\deg(\lambda) \in \mathbb{Z}\) of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}
506	  vanishes. Equivalently,
507	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker\bigl(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\bigr)\).
508	\end{theorem}
509	
510	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
511	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
512	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
513	% identity-component substrate.)
514	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
515	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
516	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
517	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
518	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
519	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
520	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
521	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
522	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
523	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
524	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
525	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
526	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
527	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
528	% everywhere."
529	\begin{proof}
530	  We establish
531	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety},
532	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}, and
533	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero} in turn, assembling
534	  the conclusions from the identity-component substrate of
535	  \cref{sec:identity_component_abstract} together with
536	  Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, Cor.~cor:sm, and Ex.~ex:jac, and
537	  Milne~\S I.1 Cor.~1.4 (commutativity) + Rmk.~III.1.4(e) (dimension).
538	
539	  \emph{Abelian-variety structure
540	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}).} We assemble the four
541	  conjuncts from four inputs.
542	
543	  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
544	  By
545	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
546	  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
547	  type by
548	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
549	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
550	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
551	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
552	  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
553	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.
554	
555	  (ii) \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
556	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
557	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
558	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
559	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
560	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
561	
562	  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
563	  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
564	  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
565	  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
566	  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
567	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
568	  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
569	  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
570	  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
571	  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
572	
573	  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
574	  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
575	  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
576	  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
577	  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
578	  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
579	  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
580	  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
581	
582	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
583	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
584	  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
585	  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
586	  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
587	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
588	
589	  \emph{Dimension equals the genus
590	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}).} By Kleiman~\S 5,
591	  Cor.~cor:sm, the inequality
592	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) holds, with
593	  equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\), in which
594	  case \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim_k H^1(C,
595	  \mathcal{O}_C)\) everywhere. Step (iv) above showed
596	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); equality therefore holds on
597	  the open identity component, and
598	  \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\)
599	  by \cref{def:genus}. This is Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): ``The
600	  dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\)''.
601	
602	  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map
603	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}).} A class
604	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
605	  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
606	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
607	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) --- precisely when
608	  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
609	  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
610	  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
611	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
612	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
613	  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
614	  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
615	  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
616	  zero on \(C\)'').
617	\end{proof}
618	
619	\section{Lean encoding}
620	\label{sec:pic0_lean_encoding}
621	
622	The Lean target file is
623	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean}, sibling to
624	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}
625	(\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}). The blueprint declarations
626	of this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
627	
628	\begin{itemize}
629	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
630	    (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is a structural
631	    constant naming the open-and-closed identity-component subscheme
632	    of a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type. The construction
633	    is the abstract substrate of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps,
634	    independent of the Picard context; it is new project material
635	    (\emph{not} yet in Mathlib).
636	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
637	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) packages the
638	    clopen-subscheme inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) (open immersion
639	    \emph{and} closed immersion) --- conclusion (a) of Kleiman~\S 5
640	    Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
641	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
642	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}) packages
643	    the group-homomorphism property of the inclusion of
644	    \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}: \(G^0\) inherits a
645	    \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\) compatible with the
646	    inclusion. Conclusion (b) of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
647	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
648	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible})
649	    packages the finite-type-ness and geometric-irreducibility
650	    conclusions of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (conclusion (c)).
651	    In Lean these are the instances
652	    \texttt{[LocallyOfFiniteType (IdentityComponent G).hom]},
653	    \texttt{[QuasiCompact (IdentityComponent G).hom]}, and
654	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible (IdentityComponent G).hom]}.
655	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
656	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}) packages the
657	    base-change-commutation conclusion (d) of Kleiman~\S 5
658	    Lem.~lem:agps~(3): for any field extension \(K/k\), the natural
659	    map \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) (in Lean,
660	    \texttt{(IdentityComponent G)\_K \(\to\) IdentityComponent (G\_K)})
661	    is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
662	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
663	    (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is the open subscheme of
664	    \(\Pic_{C/k}\) obtained by applying the
665	    \texttt{IdentityComponent} substrate to
666	    \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), inheriting the \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
667	    \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}.
668	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
669	    (\cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is the group homomorphism
670	    \(\Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\) extracting the leading coefficient
671	    of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
672	    relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on
673	    \(C\). On the disjoint-union decomposition of
674	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} the degree is the index of the
675	    component.
676	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
677	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) is the bundled
678	    abelian-variety structure on \texttt{Pic0Scheme}: the four-conjunct
679	    conjunction \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]},
680	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
681	    \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with
682	    \texttt{Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C))}.
683	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
684	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}) is the dimension
685	    formula \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\) (Milne~\S III.1,
686	    Rmk.~1.4(e)). In Lean this is the equation
687	    \texttt{Module.finrank k (Pic0Scheme C).left = AlgebraicGeometry.genus C}
688	    (precise statement via the project's Krull-dimension API on the
689	    underlying scheme).
690	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
691	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}) is the
692	    \(k\)-point-characterisation
693	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\) (Milne~\S III.1, p.~88). In Lean,
694	    \texttt{∀ λ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left,
695	    (∃ μ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left,\,
696	    μ ≫ pic0Inclusion = λ) ↔ PicScheme.degree C λ = 0}.
697	\end{itemize}
698	
699	The abstract identity-component substrate
700	(\texttt{GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} and its four
701	conclusion-specific theorems
702	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme},
703	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism},
704	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible},
705	\texttt{IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}) is new project material: it
706	is not currently in Mathlib, and the Lean skeleton is owed in a
707	follow-up iteration. The downstream
708	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety},
709	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.finrank\_eq\_genus}, and
710	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.kPoints\_iff\_kerDegree} consume Kleiman~\S 5
711	Cor.~cor:sm + Ex.~ex:jac and Milne~\S III.1, both of which currently
712	live only at the blueprint level; their Lean specialisation to the
713	smooth-proper-curve case is the main substantive step of A.3.
